message.content:
Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-10_16-33-29_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "quote for a sword with enchantment exactly 5 applies the high-enchantment surcharge"
Expected: 145 (100 base + 30 high-ench (30% of base) + 10 first-insurance = 140 + 5 fee)
Current error: Expected 145, received 105
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-10_16-33-29_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts

Apply minimal logic:
- enchantment >= 5: add 30% surcharge on item's base premium
- first-insurance also applies (item has enchantment, so it's a non-plain item)

Note: The first-insurance trigger should now apply to items with high enchantment too (not just cursed). Per spec, first insurance applies to first quote scenario regardless of item type. So the trigger should generalize.

Update so first-insurance applies whenever it's a first contract scenario AND the item has any modifier (cursed, enchantment≥5). Or simply: first-insurance applies always when isFirstContract.

But CAREFUL: existing plain item tests have customerYears=0, previousContracts=0 and expect base only (e.g., plain sword → 105G with no first-insurance). 

Reread spec: "A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge." and from integration: "each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer history." 

But the plain sword test expects 105, not 105 + 10 = 115. This means in the plain sword case (no item modifiers, perhaps representing a baseline), the first-insurance surcharge is NOT being applied. There's a contradiction.

Looking more carefully at the integration example: customer with 3 years, second quote in scenario, cursed sword ench 7, premium 160:
- 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench - 20 loyalty + 10 first insurance - 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 = 160 ✓

So both first-insurance AND follow-up contract apply on the second quote. First-insurance is per-item. Follow-up contract is per-customer-policy after the first.

But the simple "plain sword → 105" test must hold. So if we naively apply first-insurance always, it breaks. 

Looking at the test list: "first quote in a scenario applies the 10% first-insurance surcharge" is a separate test. The plain sword test from earlier (with customerYears=0, previousContracts=0) does NOT trigger first-insurance. So the input must control whether first-insurance is being applied.

The plain sword test at line 12 has customerYears: 0, previousContracts: 0 and expects 105 (no first-insurance). So first-insurance is NOT triggered by previousContracts=0 alone.

Maybe first-insurance is signaled by some field (e.g., "isFirstQuote" boolean, or contractIndex) on the input. For now, in the cursed/high-ench tests, premiums of 165 and 145 imply first-insurance is included. Without additional flags, the only signal is the existence of cursed/high-ench items.

Actually, wait - rereading more carefully. The cursed sword test expects 165 = 100 + 50 + 10 + 5. If we don't apply first-insurance, it would be 100 + 50 + 5 = 155. The test says 165. So first-insurance IS applied for cursed sword test.

But plain sword same input shape, no first-insurance → 105.

Maybe the rule is: first-insurance applies when there are item-specific modifiers (cursed or high-ench) — i.e., when the item is "first being formally assessed". Or perhaps the simpler interpretation: the first-insurance surcharge is applied per-item ONLY when the item has any "interesting" attributes (cursed, enchantment≥5, etc.). 

Actually the most pragmatic interpretation that fits ALL the existing tests: keep the current `hasCursedItem` gate, and extend it to include `hasHighEnchantmentItem`. Then for high-ench item: 100 + 30 + 10 = 140 + 5 = 145 ✓. For cursed: 100 + 50 + 10 = 160 + 5 = 165 ✓. For plain: 100 + 0 + 0 = 100 + 5 = 105 ✓.

This is the minimal interpretation - apply first-insurance only when there's an item-specific surcharge. The "first quote in a scenario" test (test 17) will further refine this.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text: I'll analyze the failing test and apply the minimal implementation.
Now I'll apply the minimal change to handle high-enchantment items.

content.text:
All 13 tests pass.

Green Phase Complete:
- Implementation: Added high-enchantment surcharge (30% of base when enchantment >= 5) and generalized first-insurance trigger to apply to any item with modifiers (cursed OR high-enchantment)
- Result: All 13 tests now pass (27 todo)
- Approach: Minimal extension of existing pattern - mirrored the cursed-item logic for high-enchantment items, and abstracted the "has modifier" check into a single predicate used in both the per-item premium path and the first-insurance gating

Verification of test expectations:
- High-ench sword: 100 base + 30 (30% high-ench) + 10 (first-insurance on 100 base) + 5 fee = 145 (matches)
- Cursed sword (existing): 100 + 50 (50% curse) + 10 (first-insurance) + 5 fee = 165 (still passes)
- Plain sword (existing): 100 + 5 fee = 105, no modifier so no first-insurance applied (still passes)

Relevant files:
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-10_16-33-29_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-10_16-33-29_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
